title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals his investigation into the murder of Eleanor Marshall, beginning with the evidence found in the room of her stepdaughter, Linda Marshall. Poirot presents findings of melted wax, burnt hair, and a book on witchcraft, suggesting Linda may have performed a superstitious ritual using a wax effigy and a needle to express a desire to kill. He notes that Linda's alibi relies on time evidence she provided herself and suggests she possessed the physical strength to have traveled from the mountain to the beach to strangle Eleanor.

When Kenneth Marshall insists on his wife's innocence, Poirot explores alternative motives such as blackmail, drug trafficking, or religious fanaticism. He proposes that Kenneth might have committed the murder with an accomplice who typed a forged letter in his room to provide an alibi. Poirot exposes that Kenneth and Rosamund Darnley lied about their whereabouts and the positioning of a typewriter to cover their actions.

Finally, Poirot turns his attention to Patrick Redfern. He argues that Eleanor Marshall went to Elfrida Bay to meet a lover, believing it to be Patrick, and that Patrick killed her after she emerged from a hiding spot. Poirot reveals that the "body" seen by witnesses was actually Patrick's accomplice, Christine, who had been painted black and was wearing a paper hat to impersonate the corpse. Poirot identifies Patrick and Christine as Edward and Christine Corey, noting they were recognized by police from a guest photograph following their involvement in the previous murder of Alice Corey.
